- **Symbolism** — A late 19th-century European movement, generally rooted in Romanticism, that was originally literary but expanded to the visual arts, where it was manifested as the painting of ideas.

- **Vienna Secession** — _“We recognize no distinction between high and low art, between art for the rich and art for the poor.
- **Viennese Actionism** — Viennese Actionism emerged in Austria in the 1960s around its founding member, [Hermann Nitsch](/artist/hermann-nitsch), whose elaborate,­ ritualisti­c performances were a shocking combination of sexuality,­ moral taboos, violence, and disgust.

## Subject Matter

213 genes.

- **Abstract Landscape** — _“My pictures are full of climates, abstract climates.
- **Adolescence** — Artworks that feature young adults—their physical appearances, emotions, and habits—have been a regular and evolving theme in the history of art.
- **Advertising and Brands** — Artworks that feature advertisements and logos.
- **African Diaspora** — Throughout the 20th century, artists (mostly of African descent) have critically addressed the historical and contemporary migration of culture, products, and bodies from the African continent.
- **Allegory** — Allegorical artworks are composed of symbols and/or personifications that convey complex ideas or narratives.
- **Alter Egos and Avatars**
- **Americana** — Artworks featuring objects or imagery associated with the United States, its history, and its culture, from Westerns and apple pie to World War II and the American flag.
- **Anatomy** — Human anatomy is the scientific study of the structure of the human organism.
- **Animal Portrait** — A category for artworks that capture the likeness of animals.
- **Animals** `AUTO` — _“Animals are such agreeable friends—they ask no questions, they pass no criticisms.” —George Eliot_

Whether pets, mythological beasts, or wild creatures, animals have always been a major subject of art and literature.
- **Anthropomorphism** — Attributing to nonhuman animals and objects human traits or forms.
- **Antiquity as Subject** — Artworks that visually reference antiquity (Greek and Roman culture), from ruins like the Colosseum to idealized depictions of the human body, such as ancient sculptures of Venus.
- **Architecture's Effects** — The built environment can shape political and social realities, as well as house memories, as the many artists who explore this theme attest.
- **Arrivals/Departures** — Artists have long used arrival and departure scenes as catalysts for dramatic action, from depictions of the Holy Family’s flight to Egypt to Antoine Watteau's [_The Embarkation for Cythera_](/artwork/jean-antoine-watteau-lembarquement-pour-cyther...
- **Artist as Ethnographer** — Artworks in which the characteristics or customs of cultural groups are described, recorded, or given order.
- **Atmospheric Landscapes** — A general category for landscapes with a particularly hazy or soft-focus look.
- **Attenuated Figure** — Representations of the human form in which a figure is stylized so as to look elongated or thinned out.
- **Bars and Restaurants** — Bars, cafes, taverns, and restaurants have been important social spaces since at least the Middle Ages, and so, unsurprisingly, there is a rich tradition of representing such spaces in works of art.
- **Bathers** — People taking baths or showers—either to clean themselves or for comfort—as well as people swimming.
- **Bedrooms and Bathrooms** — Artworks featuring depictions of bedrooms and bathrooms.
- **Bible Story** — Refers to depictions of Old and New Testament narratives, or those from other canonical Jewish or Christian texts.
- **Body Parts** — Artworks that emphasize parts of the [human body](gene/human-figure).
- **Caricatures and Parody** — _“The caricaturist plays with the likeness of his victim, and distorts it to express just what he feels about his fellow man.” –E.H.
- **Celebrity** — _“The image is one thing and the human being is another.
- **Censorship** — Artworks that take censorship—the act of prohibiting something from being read, heard, or seen—as their subject matter.
- **Childhood** — The theme of childhood has been used in art history to evoke a wide array of themes, from family life to personal histories and humanitarian concerns.
- **Circus/Carnival** — Images that feature circuses or carnivals.
- **City Scenes** — _“All great art is born of the metropolis.” —Ezra Pound_

Different from the urban panoramas of [cityscapes](/gene/cityscape), these works capture glimpses of urban life, from photographs of commuters in subway cars to abstract compositions of arc...
- **Cityscapes** — Artworks that depict cities viewed from afar.
- **Cityscapes and City Scenes** `AUTO` — Artworks that incorporate long-distance views of cities as well as intimate scenes of city life.
- **Classical Figure** — A term referring to works made in the tradition of classical Greek art, in which a figure is depicted in an idealized form, recalling that of Hercules or the goddess Venus.
- **Classical Mythology** — Artworks that depict Greek and Roman mythology.
- **Collective History** — Used in describing artworks engaged with the history of a national, ethnic, or institutional group, as opposed to the recollections of an individual.
- **Conflict** `AUTO` — Works that include scenes of personal and/or political conflict, including verbal, physical, or ideological struggles for power or agency (as in fights, wars, or arguments) or acts of opposition or antagonism (such as protests).
- **Consumerism** — Artworks engaged with the idea that the acquisition or ownership of goods is a reflection of status and self-identity.
- **Contrapposto** — An Italian term meaning “counterpose,” contrapposto refers to a now-canonical stance in which a figure stands with their entire body weight supported over one straight leg and lets their arms fall in opposite directions to create a natural-seeming...
- **Crime** — Artworks concerned in some way with illegal activities.
- **Cross-Cultural Dialogue** — Artists and works that marry the styles, artistic traditions, or imagery of disparate cultures of origin.
- **Crowds** — Any depiction of a group of over three human beings not purposefully presented as a [portrait](/gene/group-portrait).
- **Cultural Commentary** — A broad category for artworks that comment on various aspects of contemporary culture, often quite critically, as in works that focus on the misdeeds of political figures, the imperial actions of governments, or global economic inequalities.
- **Decay** — _“Ideas decompose into stones of unknowing, and conceptual crystallizations break apart into deposits of gritty reason.” —Robert Smithson_

- **Deities and Holy Figures** `AUTO` — A general category for artworks, both premodern and contemporary, that depict deities and other holy figures.

- **Diaristic** — Works—especially photographs—that keep a daily or frequent record of events, experiences and observations, and thus take on the character of a diary.
- **Digital Culture** `AUTO` — _“If it keeps up man will atrophy all his limbs but the push-button finger.” —Frank Lloyd Wright_

Artworks that focus on the vast network of websites and programs that make up the internet and how this technology has affected human behavior.

- **Engaged with Traditional Korean Art** — Contemporary or historical artworks that engage with the traditions of Korean painting, calligraphy, ceramics, textiles, or other art forms, whether through specific imagery, mediums, or techniques.

- **Equestrian** — Works in the tradition of the equestrian portrait, in which the sitter is depicted on horseback.
- **Erotic** — Works that arouse or are related to sexual desire, often incorporating [nude figures](/gene/nude).
- **Eye Contact** — Artworks that depict a subject who is making direct eye contact with the viewer.
- **Face** — Artworks whose focus is the human face.
- **Failure** — _“All art is failure.
- **Family** — Artworks that depict family scenes, ranging from joyous depictions of the [Egyptian pharaoh Akhenaten and his family](/artwork/akhenaten-nefertiti-and-the-royal-princesses), to the solemn [_Portrait of Arnolfini and his Wife_](/artwork/jan-van-eyc...
- **Femininity** — As one of many aspects of [gender](/gene/gender), femininity has had a long and complex history in art.
- **Fertility/Pregnancy/Birth** — Artworks that take as their subject fertility, pregnancy, and birth—some of the oldest subjects in art.

- **Figures in Nature** — One of the most important relationships throughout history may well be humans' relationship to nature, i.e.
- **Figures of the Art World** — Where there’s art there are people: those who make, transport, buy, sell, hang, restore, study, or spurn it.

- **Focus on the Social Margins** — _"I really believe there are things which nobody would see unless I photographed them." —[Diane Arbus](/artist/diane-arbus)_

Works that take social difference as their theme, documenting individuals or groups deemed to be marginal by society.
- **Food** — _"Tell me what you eat and I will tell you what you are." —Jean Anthelme Brillat-Savarin_

Food has long been a favored subject of artists, and at times even a medium for making art.
- **Gender** — Used in describing artworks engaged with gender, or with gender-based political rights (sometimes referred to as gender politics).
- **Glamour** — Works featuring or including glamorous people, events, or settings.
- **Globalization** — Refers to the dominance of Western—particularly American—politics, economics, and cultures around the world, or the idea that the world has been unified through new forms of technology.
- **Grotesque** — Traditionally, the fantastic combination of human and animal forms; now more generally any exaggerated (and often comic) distortion of figures or designs in bizarre, disgusting, or absurd scenes.
- **Group Portrait** — Portraits that depict at least two or more people.
- **Group of Objects** — Two-dimensional artworks depicting multiple objects whose arrangement does not conform to compositional strategies typical of traditional [still lifes](/gene/still-life).
- **Group of Portraits** — Artworks that comprise a series of portraits, like those of [Andy Warhol](/artist/andy-warhol), who depicted in individual works the same person as many as 36 different times.
- **Haze** — Works that depict haze or make a significant attempt to capture the atmosphere.
- **Hugs and Kisses** — Includes artworks that contain an embrace or a kiss between figures.
- **Human Figure** `AUTO` — Representations of the human figure are some of the earliest depictions found in art across the globe.
- **Humor** — Humor has long aided artists as a tool of social commentary, an iconic example being [William Hogarth](/artist/william-hogarth)'s “Marriage à la mode” paintings which jabbed at marriage arrangements in 18th-century English society.
- **Hybrids and Imaginary Creatures** — Imaginary creatures, from part-human hybrids to entirely fantastic beasts, have captured the attention of artists since Greek and Roman antiquity.
- **Implied Narrative** — An implied narrative arises from visual cues that create the barest impression of a story in the minds of viewers.
- **Improbable Physics** — Artworks that show figures or objects in states that seem to defy the laws of physics, such as levitation, topsy-turvy orientations, or precarious balance.
- **Individual Portrait** — _“Nothing in a portrait is a matter of indifference.
- **Industry** — Artworks depicting sites of industry and manufacturing, such as factories.
- **Infographics and Diagrams** — Shortened form of "Information graphics," which are visual representations of information.
- **Institutional Critique** — The critique of art institutions, such as museums, galleries, private collections, or publications, through artworks.
- **Interiors** — Artists have long been interested in depicting interiors, whether to demonstrate their ability to simulate three-dimensional space or to serve as settings for political, religious, or private stories.
- **Interlaced Bodies** — “For me, the nude body is like a raw material.
- **Intersectionality** — The term “intersectionality” was first coined by legal scholar Kimberle Crenshaw in relation to anti-discrimination law as a way of understanding feminism through the lens of race.
- **Isolation/Alienation** — _“Amid those scenes of solitude...the mind is cast into the contemplation of eternal things.” —[Thomas Cole](/artist/thomas-cole)_

As it is typically the fate of an artist to work alone, isolation and alienation are frequent themes throughout the...
- **Kawaii** — _Kawaii_ is a Japanese concept best translated as “cuteness.” Epitomized by [Manga](/gene/manga) comics, Hello Kitty merchandise, and the Pokémon franchise, the _kawaii_ aesthetic is a prominent aspect of Japanese [popular culture](/gene/popular-c...
- **Kitsch** — The word “kitsch” originated in the 19th century to criticize art seen as being in poor taste, or which hopelessly copied “high art” but remained mediocre or lacking in refinement.
- **Landscape and Nature Photography** `AUTO`
- **Landscapes** — _“Landscape is a creation of the mind and is intrinsically a superior art.” —Mi Fu_

- **Language** — Artworks that engage with conceptual issues surrounding language, such as translation, the relationship between words and images, and semiotics.
- **Leisure** — Spanning virtually all time periods and cultural traditions, depictions of leisure have enjoyed varying degrees of popularity and veneration in art.
- **Light as Subject** — _“Ideas are to literature what light is to painting.” —Paul Bourget_

The accurate rendering of light and shadow is one of the most important techniques in the history of Western painting, as well as a continually essential tenet of photography.
- **Line, Form, and Color** — A broad category for non-figurative artworks that foreground line, form, and color, elements that can be considered the principal building blocks of abstract art since the beginning of the 20th century.
- **Love** — Love and its many different forms is the subject of some of art history's most iconic works—and it is an often-cited reason for making art in the first place.
- **Machines** — Artworks depicting mechanical apparatuses, real or imagined.
- **Madonna and Child** — Artworks that depict baby Jesus held by his mother, Mary, characteristically as the primary focus of the artwork.
- **Manga** — _“Comics are an international language, they can cross boundaries and generations.
- **Maps/Networks** — More than tools for wayfinding, maps—like all networks—can also express the political, cultural, and religious views of their makers.
- **Masculinity** — What expectations lurk behind the phrase “Be a man!”? What does an ideal male body look like? In recent years, many artists have used their work to ask these questions, exposing the ways in which society and mass media construct concepts of mascul...
- **Math** — _"The chief forms of beauty are order and symmetry and definiteness, which the mathematical sciences demonstrate in a special degree." —Aristotle_

Artworks that depict mathematical principles, systems, or operations, or use them as a point of dep...
- **Medical/Health** — Used in describing artworks related to health or medical institutions or issues.
- **Migration** — Human migration, which encompasses diaspora, exile, immigration, and emigration, is a significant aspect of world history that has shaped the ethnic, religious, and cultural identities we know today.
- **Modes of Transportation** — Artworks depicting cars, trains, planes, buses, roads, highways, and other means of transport.
- **Mortality** — The fragile boundary between death and life has been a subject of art since the earliest recorded cultures.
- **Mourning and Commemoration** — Artworks or objects that relate to mourning or commemorating the deceased.
- **Movement** — Artworks featuring objects or human figures in motion, a subject that has been explored in countless ways in Western Art.
- **Mutated Bodies** — _“The body is comparable to a sentence that invites us to disarticulate it, so that, through a series of endless anagrams, its true contents may be recomposed.” —[Hans Bellmer](/artist/hans-bellmer)_

Applied to both contemporary and historical wo...
- **Mysticism, Shamanism, and the Occult** — Alternate realities or belief systems that fall outside of traditional religious spirituality, including but not limited to the paranormal, witchcraft, magic, alchemy, extra-sensory perception, and astrology.
- **Mythology and Religion** `AUTO` — A general category for artworks containing religious or mythological symbols or conveying religious or mythological ideas.
- **Narrative** — Applied to artworks in which a storyline, either factual or fictional, is a significant element.
- **Natural History** — Now a somewhat anachronistic term in the field of [science](/gene/science), “natural history” encompasses the study of living organisms.
- **Nature** — _“The artist has a twofold relation to nature; he is at once her master and her slave.” —Johann Wolfgang von Goethe_

To trace the history of nature in art is, essentially, to trace the history of art itself.
- **Nautical** — Nautical art includes depictions of ships at sea, the coast, maritime paraphernalia, as well as docks and port scenes.
- **Nostalgia** — Artworks recalling memories and meanings from the past in a way that might be painful—the term nostalgia is derived from the Greek words for “return” and “suffering”.
- **Nude** — Artworks that contain artistic depictions of the naked human figure.
- **Obsolescence** — Artworks concerned with the process of an object or idea ceasing to be useful and thus valuable, either due to changing fashions or technological progress.
- **Outer Space** — Works that feature images of outer space, as well as objects commonly associated with it, such as spaceships, space stations, and satellites.
- **Parties and Celebrations** — For centuries, art has been used to commemorate special events, such as coronations, important battles, and royal weddings.
- **People at Home** — A general category for depictions of human beings in the various rooms of the home.
- **Performing Arts** — Performing arts, such as dance, theater, music, and acrobatics, have long been favorite subjects for visual artists.
- **Personal Histories** — Used in describing works referring to a personal past, whether the artist’s or another individual’s, through memories, narratives, or experiences recalled or documented by the artist.
- **Personal Writing Systems** — Writing systems have existed for thousands of years, but it was not until the early 20th century that linguists—and artists—began to systematically probe the relationship between writing and communication.
- **Personification** — A personification is a human figure, often depicted with symbolic attributes, that represents an abstract idea.
- **Political** — _“Images are not innocent.
- **Political Events** — Artworks depicting political events, whether commissioned by governments to celebrate political milestones or independently created by artists to document or critique the political sphere.
- **Political Figures** — Images of presidents and politicians, or anyone holding a position in the government via election or appointment.
- **Popular Culture** — Manifest in everything from film to food labels, popular culture includes the cultural activities, products, images, and ideas embraced by the broader public, particularly as seen in mass media.
- **Portrait** `AUTO` — Loosely speaking, portraiture refers to any representation of a single [human figure](https://www.artsy.net/gene/human-figure).

- **Provocative** — _"I wanted a shark that's big enough to eat you, and in a large enough amount of liquid so that you could imagine you were in there with it." —[Damien Hirst](/artist/damien-hirst), on_ The Physical Impossibility of Death in the Mind of Someone Liv...
- **Psychoanalysis** — Developed in Vienna at the turn of the century, Sigmund Freud's theories on childhood, sexuality, and the interpretation of dreams have been hugely influential on 20th-century culture and thought.
- **Racial and Ethnic Identity** — From its increased significance at the height of European colonialism to the foundations of Enlightenment-era pseudoscience, the concept of race has been used to categorize humans along the lines of shared characteristics in order to understand hu...
- **References to Art History** — Works that visually reference a specific work of art or artist.
- **Reflections** — Reflective surfaces surround us, whether slick puddles, gleaming hubcaps, or rear view mirrors.
- **Related to Fashion** — _“One should either be a work of art, or wear a work of art.” —Oscar Wilde_

imagery related to the film industry.
- **Related to Games** — _“That little game that men have always played with one another.” —[Marcel Duchamp](/artist/marcel-duchamp), answering the question, “What is art?”_

- **Related to Hip-Hop** — Artworks or design objects that have some relationship to hip-hop or rap music, which began in the Bronx, in New York City, in 1973.
- **Related to Literature** — Artworks in which literature is a subject.
- **Related to Music** — In Western culture, from ancient Greece until the [High Renaissance](/gene/high-renaissance), music was typically considered to be a higher art form than the purely "mechanical" work of visual art because of its connections to abstract, mathematic...
- **Related to Punk** — Emerging in the 1970s as a subcultural phenomenon spanning music, fashion, and art, punk promulgated an ethos and a way of life that were anti-authoritarian, exuberantly deskilled, and staunchly DIY.

- **Related to Toys** — Kids aren't the only ones preoccupied with toys.
- **Representations of Architecture** — A category that includes images of buildings (in paintings and photographs) as well as models and sculptures of buildings.
- **Representations of Everyday Objects** — _“I am for the art of ice-cream cones dropped on concrete..
- **Royal/Court Commission** — A work of art that was commissioned by a royal family.
- **Royalty and Aristocracy** — Images of [kings](/tag/king), [queens](/tag/queen), [princes](/tag/prince), and [princesses](/tag/princess), as well as other royals and aristocrats.
- **Rural Life** — Artworks that depict pastoral life.
- **Saints** — Depictions of saints—figures officially canonized by the Roman Catholic or Eastern Orthodox church for exceptional piety in life, and who have a distinguished position in heaven as a result.
- **Scenes of Everyday Life** — A general category for artworks that depict ordinary people—whether documented or staged—in everyday life.
- **Science** — _“Study the science of art.
- **Science Fiction** — Science fiction emerged as a genre in the 19th century with works of gothic and horror literature like Mary Shelley’s _Frankenstein_.
- **Seduction and Courtship** — Throughout history, perhaps no other activity has preoccupied cultures as much as the cycle of courtship, seduction, and marriage—what Shakespeare summed up as "wooing, wedding, and repenting." It is no surprise that art the world over has depicte...
- **Self as Subject** — Artworks that take the artist as their subject, often expanding upon the traditional self-portrait's intention to capture the artist's likeness by exposing his or her inner state, body, or minutiae of daily life.
- **Self-Portrait** — _“I paint self-portraits because I am so often alone, because I am the person I know best.” —[Frida Kahlo](/artist/frida-kahlo)_

An important and highly recurring subject matter in the history of art, featuring a likeness or representation of the...
- **Sex** — _“Anytime you try to categorize sexuality, it will spit in your face.” —[Marilyn Minter](/artist/marilyn-minter)_

The complex cultural, personal, and political aspects of sex have taken on a variety of forms in art.
- **Sexual Identity** — Artworks that explore the experiences, sociopolitical struggles, and ideas associated with gay, lesbian, bisexual, and transgender identities.
- **Shadows** — Famed theorist and statesman Edmund Burke once said, "Poetry is the art of substantiating shadows, and of lending existence to nothing." Working in different eras and mediums, artists have attempted to capture this most elusive of visual phenomena...
- **Silhouettes** — The outline of a figure or object as a solid shape of a single color, usually black.
- **Skyscapes** — _“My greatest dream is the projection of light into the vast night sky, the probing of the universe as it meets the light, untouched, without obstacles—the world of space is the only one to offer man practically unlimited freedom.”—[Otto Piene](/a...
- **Southern Gothic** — Southern Gothic—a term taken from an established tradition in American Literature—is a category for artworks that feature themes and images drawn from the dark corners of the American South.
- **Spaces of the Art World** — The spaces in which to view art are as hallowed as they are numerous.
- **Sports/Athletics** — Artworks that depict or relate to sports like baseball, soccer, basketball, football, and cycling.
- **Still Life** — A depiction of inanimate subject matter, such as food, flowers, or tableware.
- **Stolen Moments** — Images and scenes involving unknown passersby seemingly captured without the permission of the subject.
- **Suburbia** — Artworks whose subject is the suburbs.
- **Surveillance** — Surveillance, in its most general sense, refers to the act of close observation, making it a natural theme for artists, long observers of others’ behaviors, to explore.
- **Symbolic Composition** — Artworks containing objects and figures that, when viewed together, imply symbolic meaning, such as in Albrecht Dürer's iconic [_Melencolia I_](/artwork/albrecht-durer-melencolia-i-b-74-m-dot-holl-75-s-dot-m-s-71) (1514), which includes a group of...
- **Talismanic** — “Talismanic” describes artworks that evoke traditional [talismans and amulets](/gene/divinatory-objects-talismans-and-amulets) but that aren’t intended for ritualistic or divinatory use.
- **Technology** `AUTO` — Before and since the [Futurists](/gene/futurism) famously declared that a roaring motor car was more beautiful than _The Winged Victory of Samothrace_ (a seminal sculpture from antiquity housed prominently in the Louvre), many artists have engaged...
- **Text** — Texts, like images, are central to representations and understandings of the world across cultures.
- **The Abject** — A term developed by feminist philosopher Julia Kristeva in her seminal essay "Powers of Horror: An Essay on Abjection" (1982), which explored the human reaction to the fragmented, decayed, or impure human body.
- **The Afterlife** — The concept of the afterlife—a realm after death in which the deceased retain some form of existence—is central to the philosophy and religion of many pre-modern cultures and finds explicit manifestation in the artwork of such cultures.
- **The American West** — The Western frontier of the United States looms large in American history.
- **The Art Market** — Artworks that focus on the art market, oftentimes with an element of critique.
- **The Artist's Studio** — Many artists have depicted art studios, transforming the physical space of artmaking into a subject worthy of art.
- **The Body** — Artworks in which the human body is the central focus or medium.
- **The City** `AUTO`
- **The Domestic and Domesticity** — Numerous artistic traditions over centuries have focused on home life.
- **The Elements** — Numerous cultures have attempted to understand the natural world as being composed of eternal or indivisible elements.
- **The Environment** — Although artists have long engaged with concepts of nature in the tradition of [landscape](/gene/landscapes), distinctly environmentalist artwork emerged in the 1960s in parallel with related social movements.
- **The Fantastic** — A general category for artworks depicting scenes, figures, or objects that appear to be imaginary, illusory, irrational, or bizarre.
- **The Mundane** — Banal, ordinary subject matter, particularly the [representation of objects](/gene/representation-of-everyday-objects) or [places](/gene/vacant-and-vacated-spaces) that are usually ignored or considered to be unremarkable.
- **The Seasons** — Throughout art history, the turning of the seasons has provided subject matter for artists.
- **The Senses**
- **The Sublime** — A term drawn from philosophy and closely associated with the wonders of [nature](/gene/nature), the sublime is meant to describe that which inspires feelings of awe, grandeur, or even anxiety and helplessness in the face of power that surpasses hu...
- **Time** `AUTO` — Artworks that deal with the subject of time.
- **Times of Day** — Artworks in which a certain time of day plays an important role.
- **Traces, Indexes, and Implied Presence** — As a type of sign, an index communicates meaning through direct physical effect—for example, a tire track represents a car; a thumbprint represents a person; and a crater represents a meteor.
- **Trauma and Struggle** — Trauma stems from the ancient Greek word “trōma,” meaning a physical, rather than psychological, wound.
- **Travel/Tourism** — Artworks that engage with the subject of travel or tourism.
- **Unsettling** — _"Fantasy, abandoned by reason, produces impossible monsters." —[Francisco de Goya](/artist/francisco-de-goya)_

Artworks with disquieting or disturbing imagery, whether of monsters or violent misfortunes, the [grotesque](/gene/grotesque) or the [...
- **Urbanization** — Used in describing artworks addressing the creation of urban spaces.
- **Utopia** — A utopia refers to an imagined, perfect society, a concept that exists in religions and cultures throughout the world, from the Christian heaven to the Tibetan earthly paradise, Shangri-La.
- **Vacant and Vacated Spaces** — Images of interior and exterior spaces in which people are absent.
- **Violence** — Artworks that address acts of physical, psychological or sexual violence.
- **Virtue and Vice** — Artworks that explore issues of morality or include depictions of good and evil, benevolence and sin.
- **Visual Perception** — Artworks that examine the subjectivity of the human visual process and its effect on art practice.
- **War and Military** — Artworks featuring images of war and soldiers, subjects with a long and rich tradition in Western art history.
- **Water** — Artworks that depict the fleeting forms and colors of water as observed from a distance, studied microscopically, or experienced from within.
- **Waterscapes** — Artworks that depict seas, rivers, and other bodies of water, as well as maritime subjects—sailors, naval battles, ports, ships, and the like.
- **Work and Workers** — Art depicting or referencing work or the workforce, whether portraits of professionals or scenes of people doing their jobs.
- **Zoomorphism** — The attribution of animal traits, forms, and behaviors and patterns deriving from animal imagery to objects and humans.

- **Aerial View** — Peril and perseverance have long been the photographer's purview, perhaps to most dizzying effect in aerial photography.
- **Album, Folio and Fan Formats** — Originally in Chinese, Japanese, and Korean art, these smaller-format works on paper or silk were created in series and intended to be viewed laying flat.
- **Altarpiece** — Altarpieces originate in the Christian tradition of placing a decorated panel on or next to an altar, the table-shaped structure that historically serves as the central focus of a house of worship.
- **Altered and Substituted Canvases** — _“While working on one of my perforated canvases, I do not want to make a painting: I want to open up space, create a new dimension for art .

- **Appropriation** — The practice, prevalent in the 1980s, of borrowing images from a range of sources both within and outside of art history and re-presenting them in new works of art.
- **Arabic/Farsi Calligraphy** — While the exact date and location of the origin of Arabic writing is debated, the earliest evidence dates to the 6th century AD.

- **Art That Plays With Scale** — Artworks that complicate one’s expected understanding of the relative size of objects.

- **Books and Portfolios**
- **Burned/Charred** — Artworks created from burned or charred materials—such as firewood—as well as works that evince the results of burning, rather than direct depictions of [fire](/tag/fire).
- **Camera Obscura** — An optical device consisting of a darkened chamber with a small circular opening to let a small amount of light pass through.
- **Caravaggesque** — Painted in the style of [Michelangelo Merisi da Caravaggio](/artist/michelangelo-merisi-da-caravaggio).
- **Carving** — Perhaps the earliest technique for creating three-dimensional forms or decoration, carving is the act of shaping, marking, or decorating wood, stone, or other materials by cutting or incising—as opposed to sculpting by building up a material like ...
- **Chance** — _“The law of chance, can be experienced only in a total surrender to the unconscious." —[Jean Arp](/artist/jean-arp)_

Beginning largely with the [Dada](/gene/dada) movement in the late 1910s, Western artists have incorporated elements of chance i...
- **Chiaroscuro** — The term _chiaroscuro_ stems from the Italian words _chiaro_ (“clear” or “bright”) and _oscuro_ (“obscure” or “dark”), and  refers to the arrangement of light and shade in a work of art.
- **Cinematic** — Artworks that recall the look of feature films, including aspects such as lighting, costumes, and sets.
- **Close-Up** — A framing technique, originating in photography (but present in film and television), which offers a very close and detailed view of an object or person.
- **Collage** — Collage generally refers to two-dimensional works created from an assemblage of different materials, although the distinction between collage and [assemblage](/gene/assemblage) (which refers to three-dimensional works) can be quite fluid.
- **Collecting and Modes of Display** — Artworks concerned with the activity of gathering and displaying objects or documents in some type of organizational scheme.
- **Color Photography** — From snapshots to [fashion photography](/gene/fashion-photography), [photojournalism](/gene/photojournalism) to art, color photography has become so ubiquitous that it is perhaps the defining visual language of today.
- **Color Theory** — Used in describing the work of artists who paint in accordance with principles regarding color mixing and the visual impact of color combinations.
- **Comic/Cartoon** — A general category for artworks that incorporate comics and cartoons.
- **Computer-Aided Manufacturing** — Objects made with the help of computerized manufacturing processes, such as 3-D printing or computer numerically controlled (CNC) routing and milling.
- **Copies and Emulations** — Before the invention of the printing press in the fifteenth century, to distribute the likeness of a work of art, artists resorted to copying.
- **Cut/Ripped** — _“My discovery was the hole and that’s it.
- **Dance** — From the Theatre of the [Bauhaus](/gene/bauhaus)’s _Triadisches Ballett_ in Weimar Germany to Japanese Butoh following World War II, dance encompasses a diverse range of rhythmic movements and performance techniques.
- **Delicate Materials** — Refers to the use of materials that can easily break or tear or that appear as though they could.
- **Densely Textured or Haptic Surface** — A general category for paintings, sculptures, and other objects with highly tactile and densely textured surface treatments.
- **Design/Architecture Study** — Preparatory studies for design objects or buildings.
- **Digital Art** — A general category for works created using digital technology, whether in the form of tangible hardware, such as computer monitors or electronics, or software, such as graphics editors, websites, and programing languages.
- **Digital Painting and Drawing** — A process in which painting or drawing takes place in a digital setting.
- **Digital Print** `AUTO` — Prints created with a computer, using highly saturated, pigment-based archival inks on paper, canvas, or vinyl.
- **Digital as Manual** — Manually created artworks that employ the visual appearance or processes of digital technology.
- **Digitally Manipulated** — Digital manipulation is the process of digitally editing an image.

- **Drawing** — _“A drawing is simply a line going for a walk.” —[Paul Klee](/artist/paul-klee)_

- **East Asian Calligraphy**
- **East Asian Ink-and-Wash Painting** — Refers to the long-standing East Asian brush painting practice involving carbon-based black ink applied to paper or silk, the same materials also used for calligraphy.
- **Embossed Paper** — Embossing is a process in which a mold is impressed into a surface, using a combination of heat and pressure, to create a raised or recessed design.
- **En plein air** — French for "in the open air," referring to painting done outdoors rather than in a studio.
- **Encaustic** — _“A beautiful woman leaves in the heart of an indifferent man an image as fleeting as a reflection on water, but in a lover’s heart, the image is fixed with fire like an encaustic painting that time will never erase.” —Plutarch_

- **Endurance Art** — Refers to artworks generally performed over a long period of time that may test the physical or emotional stamina of the artist or audience.
- **Ephemera** — The word ephemera comes from the Greek word _ephēmeros_, which means “‘lasting only a day.” Although not originally intended to be collected as art objects, some ephemera is preserved and collected for its artistic merit or historic interest.
- **Erased and Obscured** — Two-dimensional artworks that include areas of erasure, or where the artist has obscured or covered over parts of the composition to create the image.
- **Etching/Engraving** — Most likely derived from the decoration of metal armor in Northern Europe during the 15th century, etching and engraving are the two major intaglio printmaking techniques.
- **Extruded Design** — Design objects composed of or containing a fixed, cross-sectional profile that appears to have been created through forcing a material through a die.
- **Film/Video** — Films and videos began to be understood as artworks—or at least part(s) of them—in the 1950s, when artists such as [Nam June Paik](/artist/nam-june-paik-1) and Wolf Vostell began incorporating televisions into their works.
- **Focus on Materials** — A general category for characteristically abstract works for which the intrinsic properties of the chosen material largely govern the artists' process.
- **Folded Planes** — Objects created through the simple folding or bending of planar elements, from the monumental open-form sculptures of [Charles Ginnever](/artist/charles-ginnever) to Maarten Van Severen’s [LC95 Chair](/artwork/maarten-van-severen-lc95-chair)—a sin...
- **Found Footage and Found Video**
- **Fresco** — Fresco—the medium behind [Michelangelo](/artist/michelangelo-buonarroti)’s _[Sistine Chapel](/artwork/michelangelo-buonarroti-sistine-chapel-ceiling-frescoes)_ and [Raphael](/artist/raphael)’s _[School of Athens](/artwork/raphael-school-of-athens)...
- **Frontal Perspective** — Straight-on perspective is characterized by a shallow depth of field, the absence of a vantage point, and the presence of a centralized, forward-facing object.
- **Generative Art** — Art created through the use of autonomous systems, such as natural language rules, algorithms, genetic sequences, machines, or procedural interventions.
- **Gestural** — Used for paintings displaying an expressive or vigorous use of brushwork.
- **Glass and Ceramics** `AUTO`
- **Glitch Aesthetic** — Applied to artworks that incorporate, depict, or recreate technological errors—or “glitches”—such as static, spliced or distorted images, and computer bugs or viruses.
- **Gold Leaf and Gilding** `AUTO` — [Gold](/gene/gold) leaf, which is created by hammering gold into very thin sheets, is used to gild paintings, sculptures, and architectural details.
- **Handscroll** — In East Asian art, handscrolls are long, narrow, horizontal works of painting and calligraphy rendered on paper or silk that often integrate both text and images.
- **Hanging Scroll** — These works of painting and calligraphy on paper or silk are traditionally mounted for hanging upright with dowels at top and bottom.
- **Hatching** — The use of closely rendered parallel lines—most often in [drawing](/gene/drawing) and [printmaking (/gene/prints)—to create shaded areas.
- **History Painting** — A genre of [painting](/gene/painting) that was influential from the mid-[15th century](/gene/15th-century) through the [19th century](/gene/19th-century).
- **Hyperrealism** — Artworks that appear extremely realistic to the extent that they trick the eye.
- **Illustration** — A general category for artworks that straddle the line between illustration and fine art.
- **Imitated Material** — Three-dimensional works in which the artist treats a material in such a way as to obscure its inherent properties—transforming metal to look like lace, for example, or porcelain to resemble soft ribbon.
- **Immersive** — Works, typically installations, that totally envelop the viewer, often involving constructed environments and multi-sensory experiences.
- **Impasto** — Paint that stands up from the surface it has been applied to, due either to the method of its application or to the sheer quantity of paint used.
- **Installation** — A three-dimensional artwork meant to transform a viewer's experience of an interior space, through a combination of mediums, interactivity, and immersive elements.
- **Intaglio** `AUTO` — Most likely developed from the decoration of precious metals in medieval times (and with its named derived from the Italian  intagliare, or “to cut into”), intaglio printmaking refers to all printmaking in which an acid or pointed tool is used to ...
- **Intentionally Exposed Canvas** — Paintings that feature intentionally exposed, unpainted, and often unprimed canvas.
- **Interactive** — Artworks that necessitate the participation of viewers.
- **Intricately Sculpted** — Works that foreground intricate detailing, such as fine metalwork or miniature ivory carving.
- **Kinetic Sculpture** — Sculptures that include moving parts.
- **Large Brushstrokes/Loose Brushwork** — In Western painting, the use of thick, expressive brushstrokes emerged largely in the late-19th and early-20th centuries; the tendency is visible in many of [Post-Impressionist](/gene/post-impressionism) [Paul Cézanne](/artist/paul-cezanne)'s land...
- **Large-Scale Painting** — Enveloping the viewer and dominating interior spaces, large scale paintings create an impact.
- **Large-Scale Photography** — Used to describe photographs that are much larger in size than those associated with traditional film photography.
- **Large-Scale Sculpture** — Whether to inspire awe or push the technical limits of their medium, sculptors have long worked on a larger-than-life, even monumental scale.
- **Light Art** — _“Light is not so much something that reveals, as it is itself the revelation.” —[James Turrell](/artist/james-turrell)_

Artworks that use light—either artificial or natural—as a medium, whether in the form of electric light bulbs, LED, neon or f...
- **Lightbox** — Lightboxes are containers that are lit from within, most often used in doctor’s offices and artist’s studios for backlighting X-Rays and photographic negatives.
- **Lithograph** — Commonly regarded as the most difficult printmaking method to learn and master, lithography was invented in Germany in 1796.
- **Long Distance and Deep Distance** — In Chinese painting, “deep distance” describes a composition with a high horizon line and a cascading division of space into fore-, middle- and background.
- **Long Exposure** — A commonly used technique in photography that blurs the moving parts of an image or dramatically enhances light in night or dark scenes.
- **Mail Art** — Primarily associated with the work of [Ray Johnson](/artist/ray-johnson)—though it probably originated with the larger [Fluxus](/gene/fluxus) or Nouveau Réalism movements—Mail Art uses mailing and shipment services as channels through which to dis...
- **Manipulated Photography** — Photographs that have been—often dramatically—altered from their original state, either through traditional photographic printing processes or digital means such as Photoshop.
- **Manuscript** — Manuscripts are one-of-a-kind written documents.
- **Maquette** — A maquette, also known as a _bozzetto_ or _modello_, is a three dimensional object made as a preparatory study for a full-sized sculpture.
- **Mediated View** — Scenes, objects, or imagery seen through a frame, screen, or other visible layer.
- **Miniature and Small-Scale Paintings** — A general category for very small paintings.
- **Mixed-Media** — A general label for artworks made from more than one material.
- **Monochrome Painting** — The rise (and eventual dominance) of abstract art in the 20th century ushered in an era of what is called [monochromatic](/gene/monochromatic) painting.
- **Monotype/Monoprint** `AUTO` — Printmaking techniques that, unlike most others, produce editions of one instead of multiples.
- **Multiple Exposure** — The combination of two or more photographs into one image.
- **Multiple Screens** — [Videos or films](/gene/film-slash-video) that include more than one screen or projection.
- **Multiple Styles** — Artworks that contain two or more markedly different styles.
- **Murals and Wall Drawings** — Works created by applying an artistic medium directly to the wall.
- **Negative Image** — Artworks—usually photographs—featuring imagery that resembles a “negative.” In [traditional photography](/gene/traditional-photography-prints), a negative image was what was exposed on film when a photograph was taken.
- **Oil Painting** `AUTO` — Oil has been the most consistently and widely used medium in the tradition of Western painting.
- **Oil on Copper** — For a brief period of time in the 16th and 17th centuries, copper (as opposed to wood panels or canvas) was used as the ground for small oil paintings.
- **Oil on Panel** `AUTO` — Oil paintings made on wooden panels.
- **Open Form** — Sculpture or design that is not a solid mass but that literally has openings within it, where lines and planes often replace solid volumes or enclosing surfaces and thus the open (negative) space becomes a major component of the work.
- **Organic Material** — Artworks that employ living (or formerly living) organisms as material, from feathers, fur, bones, or bodily substances to dirt, twigs, plants, and food.
- **Outdoor Art** — Artworks situated outdoors.
- **Painting** — _"Painting completed my life."—[Frida Kahlo](/artist/frida-kahlo)_

Painting—for many, the answer to the question "What is art?"—could be as old as humanity itself.
- **Painting and Writing Tools** — For painting, calligraphy and writing tools such as seals (chops), ink stones, ink cakes, brushes, brush holders, arm rests, etc., that were and are still collected by connoisseurs.
- **Panorama** — The term “panorama” is understood today as an extended view of a location depicted in two dimensions, though it originated in the [18th century](/gene/18th-century) and meant something quite different.
- **Paper Cut-Outs** — Since the invention of paper in China in the 1st century BCE, paper cut-outs have been utilized by a long line of craftsmen, folk artists, and fine artists.
- **Passage** — A French term (pronounced “pahsazh”) for a painting technique characterized by small, intersecting planes of patchlike brushwork that blend together to create an image (art historians tend to retain the French so as to avoid confusion with the amb...
- **Pastel** — Pastel refers to a drawing tool made of colored powder molded into a stick.
- **Performance Art** — A genre of art-making in which the artwork is the artist performing—usually live but often in a film or video.
- **Photogram** — _"[T]he essential tool of the photographic process is not the camera but the light-sensitive layer." —[Laszlo Moholy-Nagy](/artist/laszlo-moholy-nagy)_

Photograms (or "camera-less photographs") push photography to its logical extreme; they result...
- **Photograph as Material** — Crumpled, drenched, folded, or torn: photographs, when physically manipulated, can become the raw material for three-dimensional works of art, a potential explored by many contemporary artists today.
- **Photographic Source** — Artworks in which the subject and composition are inspired by or directly derived from a source photograph, whether found or taken by the artist.
- **Photographs of Surfaces** — _"The camera should be used for a recording of life, for rendering the very substance and quintessence of the thing itself, whether it be polished steel or palpitating flesh.” —[Edward Weston](/artist/edward-weston)_

Steel, flesh, billboards, or ...
- **Photography** `AUTO` — _"Photography helps people to see."  —[Berenice Abbott](/artist/berenice-abbott)_

Arguably the most popular medium in contemporary art, photography was invented in 1839.
- **Photojournalism** — Photographs that convey news through photographs and have been (or were meant to be) published in newspapers, magazines or online news sources.
- **Photomontage** — Russian artist [Varvara Stepanova](/artist/varvara-fedorovna-stepanova) defined photomontage in 1928 as “the assemblage of the expressive elements from individual photographs.” A technique best known for its close ties to [Dada](/gene/dada), photo...
- **Photorealistic** — _"Perhaps because I’m sorry for the photograph, because it has such a miserable existence even though it is such a perfect picture, I would like to make it valid, make it visible—just make it […] That is why I keep painting on and on from photogra...
- **Picassoesque** — Artworks, generally painting, that resemble or emulate the work of [Pablo Picasso](/artist/pablo-picasso), arguably one of the most influential artists of the 20th century.
- **Polychrome Sculpture** — The word “polychrome” derives from the Greek “polu-” (meaning many) and “khrōma” (color).
- **Poster** — Large printed pictures—usually a combination of images and words—that advertise events or messages.
- **Poured** — A technique in which paint, or another liquid medium, is poured to create a flowing, (most often) abstract composition.
- **Printed Matter** — _“A newspaper that you're not reading can be used for anything.” —[Robert Rauschenberg](/artist/robert-rauschenberg)_

From scrapbooks to baseball card collections, ticket stubs saved and newspaper articles clipped, printed matter carries not only...
- **Prints** `AUTO` — A general category for works, typically on paper, that consist of an impressed or stamped image.
- **Process-Oriented** — Artworks in which the act of creating a work almost seems to take precedence over the finished  product.
- **Projection** — As Greek myth would have it, cast shadows created the world’s first artwork: the daughter of a famous potter [traced the outline of her lover’s shadow](/artwork/joseph-wright-the-corinthian-maid) on the wall as he departed on a journey, and from t...
- **Pulled, Scraped, and Scratched** — Artwork that are made with paint that has been pulled or scraped across a two-dimensional support, or the technique of scratching or incising the surface.
- **Recycled** — A category that encompasses the use of garbage, discarded materials, and thrown-away objects in art.
- **Reduced-Scale Architecture** — In addition to architects who create small-scale models of buildings in preparation for their full-size constructions, artist and artisans throughout history have produced miniaturized versions of architectural structures.
- **Relief** — A type of [sculpture](/gene/sculpture) in which images or forms project out from a flat surface.
- **Rembrandtesque** — Recalling the style and techniques of [Rembrandt van Rijn](/artist/rembrandt-van-rijn).
- **Repetition** — _"I like things to be exactly the same over and over again" —[Andy Warhol](/artist/andy-warhol)_

A general category for artworks that repeat images, elements, or gestures.
- **Research as Art** — Works that foreground extensive research into individual, social, cultural, or scientific objects of inquiry.
- **Restored and Repurposed** — Artworks or objects whose original form has been significantly altered as a result of heavy restoration or in an effort to repurpose it for a new use.
- **Screen Format** — Refers to historical and contemporary works that use traditional East Asian screen formats.
- **Sculptural Use of Paint**
- **Sculpture** — _“Sculpture is the art of the hole and the lump.” —[Auguste Rodin](/artist/auguste-rodin)_

From the Parthenon frieze and the Easter Island _maoi_ to the Chinese Terracotta Warriors and the prehistoric _Venus of Willendorf_, sculpture is a medium ...
- **Sculpture-Based Photography** — Photographs of a sculpture—or a more complex installation—that has been built primarily for the purpose of being photographed and most often does not exist outside of the documentation.
- **Sfumato** — In painting, a technique of almost transparent shading that allows transitions between different lines, color and tones to be blurred to the extent that they appear to almost (to use the literal meaning of the term) evaporate like smoke.
- **Shallow Depth of Field** — _"Every period has its own optical focus." —Laszlo Moholy-Nagy_

&nbsp;

Using a shallow depth of field in photography results in images in which not all fields (fore-, middle-, and background) are in focus.
- **Silkscreen** — Silkscreen is another name for the screenprinting technique, a printmaking process in which an ink-blocking stencil is applied to a screen, allowing ink that is wiped across the screen to selectively pass through to a printing surface.
- **Site-Specific Art** — The idea, which came to prominence in the 1960s, that the physical location and surroundings of an artwork are inseparable from its identity.
- **Snapshot Aesthetic** — _“I am a passionate lover of the snapshot, because of all photographic images it comes closest to truth […] The snapshooter's […] pictures have an apparent disorder and imperfection, which is exactly their appeal and their style.
- **Soft Sculpture** — Sculptures composed of soft materials, such as rubber, latex, or cloth.
- **Sound Art** — Sound Art refers to modern and contemporary artworks that foreground sound.
- **Splattered/Dripped** — The use of a dripped or splattered art material&mdash;usually paint&mdash;characteristically in paintings.
- **Spray Paint** — Invented in 1949 by paint salesman Ed Seymour to apply aluminum coating to radiators, spray paint is paint that sprays onto any surface (walls, wood, metal, glass, plastic, or canvas) from an aerosol can.
- **Staged Photography** `AUTO` — Photographs that capture staged or artificially constructed scenes made only for the purpose of photography.
- **Stains/Washes** — Two-dimensional artworks—primarily paintings and drawings—that include stains and washes.
- **Study** — Works in any medium of the visual arts that explore a subject or are preliminary to a separate, more finalized work; more finished than sketches.
- **Suspended/Hanging** — The technique of hanging an artwork, typically sculptural, from the ceiling, or suspending a series of objects in space, giving the effect of them floating in air.
- **Tempera** — Though the term literally means any mixture of pigment and medium (the two basic parts of any paint), tempera primarily refers to egg tempera, a kind of paint used since antiquity in which egg is the binding medium for the pigment.
- **Time-Lapse Photography and Film** — A cinematography technique in which a changing scene is captured in a series of frames shot at set intervals.
- **Tondo** — Circular paintings and relief sculptures.
- **Topographic Photography** — Technique in which a scene&mdash;usually a landscape&mdash;is photographed as if it were being surveyed from afar, practiced most famously by the 1970s [‘New Topographics’](/gene/new-topographics) photographers, including Robert Adams, Lewis Baltz...
- **Traditional Photographic Techniques** `AUTO` — Photographs produced using printing processes developed before 1875, or during the nascent phases of the medium.
- **Traditional Scroll Painting** `AUTO` — Chinese, Korean, or Japanese paintings, often depicting landscapes or scenes from daily life or literary or historic narratives and generally created using an ink brush on scrolled paper, although the methods, styles, and materials varied widely a...
- **Transfer** — A general label for the technique of transmitting an image from one surface to another.
- **Trompe l’oeil** — _"Everything we see hides another thing; we always want to see what is hidden by what we see." —[René Magritte](/artist/rene-magritte)_ 

Translating literally from French as to deceive or trick the eye, trompe l'oeil describes works whose subject...
- **Typography** — Artworks that include a particular typeface (typically borrowed from an established body of available typefaces) or artworks that comment on the subject of typography in general.
- **Typologies** — Artworks or series of artworks illustrating a classification system or set of types.
- **Use of Traditional Techniques** — Contemporary art and design works that make use of traditional techniques, a tendency that spans global cultures and includes a wide range of experiments with materials and imagery.
- **Virtual and Augmented Reality**
- **Wall Sculpture and Installation** — Walls have served as the foundation for paintings, reliefs, and mosaics since the earliest artistic expressions in prehistoric caves.
- **Watercolor** — Watercolor painting involves the mixture of pigment, water, and an adhesive agent, typically applied to paper but sometimes silk or vellum.
- **Website** — A category for artworks that consist of code and a user experience on a web browser.
- **Woodcut and Linocut** `AUTO` — A printmaking technique in which an image is carved into a block of wood or linoleum; the surface of the block is then inked with a roller and printed, leaving an image only where the block has not been carved away.
- **Work on Paper** — Includes manuscripts, prints, drawings, collages, and paintings on paper.

## Visual Qualities

74 genes.

- **Abstract Illusionism** — In the mid-1970s, art critic Barbara Rose identified a trend in painting in which abstract works were rendered with a sense of perspective, depth, and shadow.
- **Allover Composition** — “…all hierarchical distinctions have been, literally, exhausted and invalidated.”—Clement Greenberg
 
The term “all-over picture” was first used by art critic Greenberg in his 1948 essay “The Crisis of the Easel Picture” to describe the emergence ...
- **Angular** — Refers to two- and three-dimensional artworks characterized by the prevalence of acute angles.
- **Arabesque/Scroll** — Describes objects or compositions characterized by a predominance of curling, scroll-shaped tendrils or winding curves, also called volutes, S-scrolls, or C-scrolls.
- **Asymmetrical** — Applied to design objects with multiple components that do not mirror each other's shape or composition.
- **Balance** — Painters, sculptors, and architects have engaged with balance in their works to present forms that have a tenuous distribution of weight, threatening to become unstable by outside forces.
- **Biomorphic** — The category of biomorphic art is large and diverse, drawing connections between nature-inspired objects across time periods and geographies.
- **Black and White** `AUTO` — Artworks, including photographs, that are either all black, all white, or a combination of the two.
- **Blown-Off-Roof Perspective** — Derived from the Japanese name _fukinuki yatai_, literally, “roof blown off,” a type of perspective that shows a bird's eye view into a dramatically sloping picture plane.
- **Blurred** — Category for two-dimensional artworks that include blurs—areas in which images are less distinct—in them.
- **Bright and Vivid Colors** — The use of attention-grabbing or intense color has persisted across time and cultures, despite coming in and out of fashion.
- **Bulbous** — A term applied to bulging, swollen or bulb-shaped forms and objects.
- **Calligraphic** — Artworks that employ or resemble the lines of calligraphy.
- **Color Gradient** — A term used in computer graphics to describe a fluid range of color filling an area.
- **Columns and Totems** — Tall, upright, and vertical sculptures and monuments.
- **Contour Line** — A contour line defines the outline of a form, as well as interior structure, without the use of shading.
- **Cracked** — When [Marcel Duchamp](/artist/marcel-duchamp)’s _[The Large Glass](/artwork/julian-wasser-marcel-duchamp-standing-with-the-large-glass-duchamp-retrospective-pasadena-art-museum)_ accidentally cracked while being transported from the Brooklyn Museu...
- **Creased Crinkled Wrinkled** — From the wind-blown, ruffling drapery of the [Winged Victory of Samothrace](/artwork/victoire-de-samothrace-winged-victory-of-samothrace) to the crushed automobile sculptures of [John Chamberlain](/artist/john-chamberlain), creases, crinkles, and ...
- **Crystalline and Geological Forms** — In a convergence of art and [science](https://www.artsy.net/gene/science), crystalline and geological forms can create a range of beautiful visual effects, from rough or rustic unworked stone to recurring linear crystal [patterns](https://www.arts...
- **Curvilinear Forms** — Objects characterized by curving lines and shapes, such as arcs or circles.
- **Dark Colors** — Works determined to be dark in color, not necessarily in mood or outlook.
- **Dense Composition** — Similar to “horror vacui,” a Latin-derived term for “fear of empty spaces,” compositions in this category cover the entire surface of a work with [highly-detailed](/gene/highly-detailed) visual content.
- **Divisionist** `AUTO` — Divisionism, traditionally speaking, refers to the style developed by [Neo-Impressionist painters](/gene/neo-impressionism) in an effort to compose images through the use of small units of pigment (dots, dashes, etc.), with an emphasis on the opti...
- **Dotted** — Artworks that feature repeated small spots or circles.
- **Dynamism** — _"We declare that the splendor of the world has been enriched by a new beauty: the beauty of speed.
- **Earth Tones** — Includes artworks that employ colors which occur in nature including unsaturated greens, browns and clay pigments like umber, ochre, and sienna.
- **Emphasis on Linear Perspective** — Works in which one-point linear perspective—wherein all aspects of a composition are laid out according to a mathematically derived vanishing point—plays a central part.
- **Extreme Angle** — "_Contradictions of perspective..
- **Flatness** — A category for two-dimensional artworks that include flattened figures or objects, or have shallow depth.
- **Fractal-like / Kaleidoscopic** — Artworks with an incredible level of compositional complexity, often achieved through the use of intricate, all-over patterns that disorient or cause the eye to flit about, as with the infinitely repeating patterns of fractals or kaleidoscopes.
- **Fragmented Geometry** — Fragmented planes, the explosion of linear perspective—this vision of the world put forth by [Pablo Picasso](/artist/pablo-picasso) and [Georges Braque](/artist/georges-braque), beginning around 1909, continues to inspire how contemporary artists ...
- **Geometric** — Artworks that foreground the precise rendering of lines, angles, and shapes.
- **Glittery** — Although gems and stones have been used as far back as 40,000 BC to add shine and sparkle to cave paintings, the modern material we know as "glitter" was not invented until 1934.
- **Grid** — A general category for both two- and three-dimensional artworks incorporating a grid or lattice structure in some fashion.
- **Hard-Edged** — A term originating in the late 1950s for abstract paintings characterized by sharply defined geometric areas of flat colors conveying little or no depth, such as works by [Ellsworth Kelly](/artist/ellsworth-kelly), [Kenneth Noland](/artist/kenneth...
- **Highly Detailed** — Works whose visual information is crisp, meticulous, and executed with precision.
- **Highly Ornamented** — Works characterized by a high level of ornamentation.
- **Iridescence/Opalescence** — Surfaces that seem to change color when viewed from different angles.
- **Irregular Curvilinear Forms** — Works featuring rounded [geometric](/gene/geometric) forms (such as circles, ellipses, parabolas, and curves) that are executed with an irregular edge or line.
- **Irregular Linear Forms** — A term for artworks that emphasize the use of mostly straight lines and shapes, though drawn without the help of a straight-edge.
- **Layered Images** — Artworks with distinct overlays of visual imagery.
- **Linear Forms** — A term for artworks that foreground straight lines and discrete shapes as if drawn with a ruler, without indication of the artist’s hand.
- **Marbleized** — Artworks or design objects whose entire surface, or parts within it, appear to be made out of polished [marble](/gene/marble).
- **Metallic** — Works that have a metallic sheen.
- **Mimicking Ruined Materials** — Works in this category mimic ruins in appearance or bear surface qualities that imitate decayed matter (as opposed to artworks that depict [ruins](/tag/ruins)).
- **Mirrored** — Artworks and design objects whose surfaces include mirrors.
- **Molecular** — Used in describing artworks containing forms resembling molecules, atoms, or other microscopic particles.
- **Pastel Colors** — Generally refers to light colors with medium saturation.
- **Patinated and Oxidized** `AUTO` — Patination and oxidation are chemical reactions that change the surface appearance of metals like bronze or copper, creating a green, pigmented “patina,” or “verdigris.” This chemical change occurs naturally as metal ages, as with [ancient ritual ...
- **Patterns** — Patterns—designs composed of repeated motifs—shape the world around us; their arrangements are integral to art as much as nature.
- **Piles/Stacks** — Artworks that are partly or entirely piled or stacked.
- **Pixelated** — Artworks and design objects that include some area(s) in which pixels are made intentionally visible.
- **Primary Abstraction** — A general category for both contemporary and historical abstract works featuring basic shapes and colors and defying figurative associations, such as [Kasimir Malevich](/artist/kasimir-malevich)’s Suprematist paintings and [Minimalist](/gene/minim...
- **Primary Colors** — Artworks that feature the purest forms of the colors red, blue, or yellow.
- **Psychedelic** — Refers to art in which the “look” is based on psychedelic experiences that characteristically stem from the use of drugs like LSD, psilocybin, and mescaline.
- **Radiating Lines** — Art and design in which the overall composition is defined by radial stripes emanating from a single or multiple points.
- **Raw versus Finished** — Works that combine opposing treatment of materials, with one highly finished and one left raw.
- **Rough** — Artworks that have a rough or rough-hewn­ exterior surface.
- **Scattered Composition** — Scattered compositions contain multiple elements irregularly distributed across an artwork’s surface in many directions.
- **Sharp/Prickly** — Three-dimensional artworks and design objects with distinct points or jagged edges that look like they could cut or tear something.
- **Shiny/Glossy** — Artworks and design objects that have a smooth, lustrous surface.
- **Single Object**
- **Slender** — Sculptures and Design objects that are tall, thin, or elongated.
- **Slumped/Draped** — Artworks that are slumped or draped in form.
- **Smooth Surface** — Three-dimensional artworks and design objects with a smooth surface, either by virtue of the natural texture of the materials used or as the result of a process of finishing or manipulating these materials.
- **Sparse** — Markedly simple or unadorned.
- **Stained Glass Effect** — The use of thick, dark lines to enclose planes of vivid color.
- **Striped** — A general category for both two- and three-dimensional artworks incorporating stripes in some fashion.
- **Symmetrical** — Symmetrical artworks are those in which the entire work or certain elements within it include imagery that is reflected in a common axis.
- **Tangled Forms** — Two- and three-dimensional artworks that include (or are characterized by) closely meshed, overlapping, and typically curvilinear lines.
- **Transparent/Translucent Medium** — Artworks that viewers can see through, or almost see through.
- **Viscous Forms** — Used mostly for three-dimensional sculptures and design objects that appear to be melting, oozing, or dripping.
- **Woven/Perforated** — Artworks and design objects that include some parts comprised of interlacing strands of various types of material and/or portions that are perforated.
- **Wrapped** — Wrapping an object inherently hides its features to produce a sense of mystery while simultaneously highlighting its surface and form, aesthetic effects that many artists have harnessed over the years.

## Materials

39 genes.

- **Aluminum** — Artworks and design objects created from aluminum.
- **Assemblage** — The technique of creating three-dimensional artworks by combining objects--both natural and manufactured, usually from the world outside art making and in their original form.
- **Bone** — Artworks and design objects made in whole or in part from bone, including any part of the skeleton of an [animal](/gene/animals), such as a [skull](/tag/skull).
- **Brass** `AUTO` — Functional objects, sculptures and other three dimensional works made of brass.
- **Bronze** — Bronze, an alloy of copper, tin, and sometimes lead, has long been a favored medium for sculpture and decorative objects around the world.
- **Cardboard** — Artworks that are, in whole or in part, created out of cardboard.
- **Ceramic** `AUTO` — _“Earth, water, air and fire so simple, but so complex.” —Unknown_

Ceramics are made by shaping water, earthen powders, and clay into forms, which are then fired at high temperatures.
- **Chalk** — While today most often associated with sidewalks and classrooms, chalk has been used as an art-making medium for thousands of years in its naturally occurring colors—red, white, and black (manufactured chalks did not appear until the 18th century).
- **Chrome-Plated** `AUTO` — While this category primarily concerns [design objects](/gene/design), it applies to any works featuring a chromium layer that has been electroplated onto all or part of its [metal](/gene/metal) or [plastic](/gene/plastic) surface(s).
- **Concrete** — Beginning in Rome in the 1st century BC, concrete came into use as a key technological breakthrough for the construction of bridges and aqueducts.
- **Enamel** — A glass or vitreous paste that has historically been used to coat and decorate metals and ceramics.
- **Found Objects** — Used to group works that include, or are predominantly constituted of, found objects.
- **Gemstone** — Because of their scarcity and beauty, gemstones such as diamonds, pearls, emeralds, sapphires, and rubies have been used for centuries to indicate wealth, status, and power in visual culture.
- **Glass as Material** `AUTO` — _"All the effects of glass are marvelous… it must be used and kept in mind as an example of the life of man and of the things of this world which, though beautiful, are transitory and frail." —Vannoccio Biringuccio_

Glass—a hard, transparent mate...
- **Gold** — Artworks, design objects, or architectural works that are composed, at least in part, of gold.
- **Inlay** — Refers to materials, such as mosaic tiles, that have been embedded or sunken into the surface of an object to create a design.
- **Ivory** — Artworks and design objects that include what is technically known as dentine, a material that comes from the tusks of elephants, as well as other mammals.
- **Leather/Hide**
- **Marble** `AUTO` — Artworks and design objects wholly or partially composed of marble, a type of limestone that has recrystallized due to extreme heat.
- **Metal** `AUTO` — A general category for artworks, typically sculptures, and design/decorative objects made of metal.
- **Mosaics** — Mosaic, the art of arranging small pieces of material to create a larger image, is one of the oldest and most durable artistic techniques, having its start as wall decoration in ancient [Mesopotamia](/gene/mesopotamian-art).
- **Natural Fibers** — Design objects created using threads derived from plants and animals.
- **Neon and Fluorescent Light** — Discovered in the late 19th century, neon light results from charged, glowing particles of the element neon and other gases in glass tubes.
- **Plaster** — Commonly used to create sculptures, plaster is made by mixing water, lime, gypsum, sand, and other strengthening materials, such as animal hair.
- **Plastic** — Invented in the mid-19th century, plastic is one of the most prominent materials used in day-to-day life, and thanks to 20th century adopters, now in art.
- **Porcelain** `AUTO` — A general category for works (mainly vessels and sculptures) made from clay-based products hardened by heat.
- **Resin** — Resin—which can be derived organically from plants or produced synthetically in the lab—is a liquid material that can permanently harden into a transparent solid form.
- **Silver** — Artworks or design objects that are wholly or partly made of silver.
- **Stained Glass** — Almost two millennia ago in ancient Rome, artisans started using stained glass to activate religious and secular spaces with the power of colored light.
- **Steel** — While the use of steel in [armor, weaponry](/gene/arms-and-armor), and decorative objects dates back to antiquity in both Europe and Asia, the large-scale production of steel was made possible by technological advances only in the late 19th century.
- **Stone** `AUTO` — A general category for artworks, design objects, or architectural works made from rock, such as [marble](/gene/marble), granite, or limestone.
- **Taxidermy** — In 1959, [Robert Rauschenberg](/artist/robert-rauschenberg) created [Canyon](/artwork/robert-rauschenberg-canyon), one of his most iconic combine paintings, for which he affixed a taxidermied [bald eagle](/tag/bald-eagle) to a canvas.
- **Terracotta**
- **Use of Common Materials** — Identifies artworks composed of common or everyday materials, such as the house paint that [Frank Stella](/artist/frank-stella) employed in his late 1950s "Black Paintings.".
- **Use of Ephemeral Materials** — Refers to works that employ short-lived materials, a significant aspect of the practice of many modern and contemporary artists who reject more traditional media, such as oil paint, marble, and bronze, which were chosen for their ability to stand ...
- **Use of Paper** — An artwork that incorporates paper as a sculptural or craft medium for expression, as opposed to as a canvas.
- **Use of Precious Materials** `AUTO` — Most often used for [jewelry](/gene/jewelry) design, precious materials—such as [gold](/gene/gold), [silver](/gene/silver), [ivory](/gene/ivory), and [gemstone](/gene/gemstone)—are also employed to enrich works of art and architecture.
- **Use of Vintage Imagery** — Late 19th-century botany books, vintage pin-ups, antique maps, circus posters, and etchings of once-novel machines like the phonograph or stereoscope—these relics of past visual cultures fascinate many artists working today.
- **Wood** — A general category for art and design works in which wood is a primary material.

## Time Periods

20 genes.

- **1000–1400 CE** `AUTO` — A general category for any artwork created between 1000 and 1400.
- **15th Century** `AUTO` — The 15th century marks a turning point in art history.
- **16th Century** `AUTO` — A general category for any artwork produced between 1500 and 1599.
- **17th Century** `AUTO` — The 17th century was marked by increasingly robust global networks of trade, which brought a great deal of exchange of artistic objects and the global spread of styles.
- **1860–1969** — All art, design, decorative art, and architecture produced from roughly 1860 to 1970.
- **18th Century** `AUTO` — A category for any work of art created during the 18th century, which witnessed two of the most influential periods of the modern age: the Enlightenment and the French Revolution.
- **1900–1917** `AUTO` — The turn of the twentieth century brought a spirit of experimentation to Europe, as artists looked for new ways to break with artistic conventions.

- **1970s** `AUTO` — A general category for any artwork, design object, or architectural work created during the 1970s.

- **21st Century** `AUTO` — Over the past decade, art has taken an incredible variety of forms.
- **500–1000 CE** — A general category for any artwork created between 500 and 1000 CE.
- **Ancient Art** — Between approximately 5000 BC and 300 AD, “advanced” civilizations (generally, those with written language) thrived in Mesopotamia, Egypt, Greece, Sumeria, Akkadia, Mexico, Rome, Japan, China, and India.
- **Late 19th Century** `AUTO` — A general category for works created between 1870 and 1900.

## Tableware, Vessels, and Objects

17 genes.

- **Artist Merchandise and Multiples** — The art multiple is an object reproduced by an artist in numerous copies.
- **Bowl** `AUTO` — A round dish used for food, liquid, or ornamentation.
- **Clock** `AUTO`
- **Container**
- **Door** — Doors—not depictions of them, in [paintings](/gene/painting) or [photographs](/gene/photography), but the physical objects.
- **Flatware** — Forks, knives, spoons, or any utensils used for eating.
- **Frame** — A general category for antique and modern functional frames—cases that are intended to support, protect, or add a decorative element to another work of art for display.
- **Glass Objects** — A category for objects composed of [glass](/gene/glass), also referred to as “glass art” or “studio glass,” as distinct from the mass-produced, functional glass used in buildings, furniture, and even some sculpture.

- **Objets de Vertu** — A category for tiny decorative pieces noted for their fineness of material and craft, often reflecting considerable effort and finesse on the part of the maker.

- **Tableware** — The dishware used for setting a table or serving food, exclusive of flatware.
- **Tool** — Tools—not depictions of them, in [paintings](/gene/painting) or [photographs](/gene/photography), but the physical objects.
- **Tray** `AUTO` — Trays—not depictions of them, in [paintings](/gene/painting) or [photographs](/gene/photography), but the physical objects.
- **Vase** `AUTO` — A container that is deeper than it is wide, and which is either for decoration or for holding plants and flowers.
- **Vessel** — [Bowls](/gene/bowl), [vases](/gene/vase]), and other hollow containers characteristically used for liquids.
- **Wallpaper** — Decorative paper that is applied in strips to the walls of a room.
